results.proposal_summary: The IKI Thematic Call 2023, funded by the German Federal Government through the International Climate Initiative (IKI), supports large-volume projects addressing 13 thematic priorities spanning climate mitigation, adaptation, and biodiversity conservation in developing and emerging countries. Grants range from EUR 5 million to EUR 20 million per project, with one or two projects selected per thematic priority. Eligible applicants include German and international grant recipients, as well as implementing organisations of the German Federal Government such as GIZ and KfW; international organizations such as the ILO are not explicitly listed as eligible. The first mandatory submission deadline for project outlines was 27 February 2024, which has passed. Implementation takes place in ODA-eligible partner countries across developing and emerging economies globally.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The IKI Thematic Call 2023 focuses primarily on climate mitigation, biodiversity, and environmental protection, with no thematic priorities directly addressing labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work, which are core to the ILO mandate.

results.exclusion_reason: Deadline is past: the submission period ran from 01 November 2022 to 21 February 2023, which has passed. Additionally, the call explicitly lists eligible implementing organisations (German and international grant recipients, contractors as implementing organisations of the German Federal Government) without including international organisations such as the ILO as a distinct eligible category in the standalone grant track, though international intergovernmental organisations are mentioned in the legal basis context.
results.classification_summary: The IKI Thematic Call 2022 is ineligible because the mandatory project outline submission deadline of 21 February 2023 has long passed. The selection process has been completed and all participants notified. The call is therefore closed, making further application impossible.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The funding announcement states participation is open to 'German and international grant recipients as well as to contractors in the form of implementing organisations of the German Federal Government.' Section 2.3 notes that where grants are issued to multilateral development banks and international intergovernmental organisations, model agreements apply, which implies intergovernmental organisations can receive grants. ILO as an international intergovernmental organisation could arguably fit, but the category is not explicitly enumerated as a separate eligible applicant type.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The funding announcement explicitly states projects receive funding through non-reimbursable grants ('Zuwendungen') under sections 23 and 44 of the Federal Budget Code. Grant agreements are referenced throughout the document.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The funding volume is stated as EUR 10 million to a maximum of EUR 20 million per project, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The project submission period ran from 01 November 2022 to 21 February 2023. The page states 'The selection of project ideas from Thematic Call 2022 has been completed.' The deadline is far in the past relative to today (2026-04-23).

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The call is described as an 'ideas competition' for grant funding, not a procurement tender or request for services/consultancy.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No mandatory co-financing requirement is stated in the available text. The call mentions leveraging funds but does not impose a co-financing condition on applicants.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body (e.g., GEF, Green Climate Fund) is required. The call sets requirements for implementing organisations but not formal accreditation with a third-party body.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The call requires a lead organisation in consortia but does not explicitly restrict consortium leadership to non-UN entities. Selected projects include UN-Habitat and UNECA as consortium members, with some as lead organisations.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The call is open to both German and international implementing organisations, and selected consortia include organisations from multiple countries. Eligibility is not restricted to Germany-based entities only.

results.proposal_summary: The IKI Thematic Call 2022 was a joint ideas competition organised by the German Federal Ministries BMWK, BMUV, and AA under the International Climate Initiative (IKI), providing grants of EUR 10–20 million per project in ODA-eligible countries across Asia, Africa, Latin America, and the Pacific. The call targeted 12 thematic priorities spanning climate mitigation, adaptation, and biodiversity conservation, including just energy transition, agri-photovoltaics, deforestation-free supply chains, freshwater protection, and climate-related loss and damage. Eligible implementing organisations included German and international entities (NGOs, research institutions, international organisations, private sector) forming consortia. The submission period ran from 1 November 2022 to 21 February 2023 and has since closed; 14 of 196 submitted outlines were selected for the second phase. No co-financing requirement was imposed, and the call is now closed with no further applications accepted.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses primarily on climate action, biodiversity conservation, and energy transition rather than ILO core mandates of labour standards, employment, and social protection, though limited just transition and skills development elements are mentioned as secondary project components.

results.exclusion_reason: Deadline is past: the project submission period ran from 11 November 2020 to 10 March 2021, which is well before today's date of 2026-04-23. Additionally, the call explicitly lists eligible implementing organisations (Section 6) and restricts eligibility to specific entity types that do not include UN agencies or international organisations such as the ILO.
results.classification_summary: The Thematic Call 2020 is a closed call whose submission period ended on 10 March 2021, rendering all deadlines firmly in the past. The funding information document (Section 6) defines eligible implementing organisations with a strict and exhaustive list that does not include UN agencies or intergovernmental organisations such as the ILO. Both the past deadline and the ILO eligibility criteria independently disqualify this opportunity.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: Section 6 of the funding information document sets out requirements for implementing organisations/consortia for funding priorities 1 to 11. The eligible entity types listed are specific and exhaustive and do not include UN agencies or international organisations such as the ILO. Although some selected consortia included UN bodies (e.g. FAO, UNESCO), the call text itself does not list international organisations as an eligible category in the defining section.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The call provides non-reimbursable grant funding from the IKI/BMU, with funding volumes of 5 to 30 million euros per project, constituting a non-repayable contribution.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The funding amount ranges from 5 million to a maximum of 30 million euros per project, far exceeding the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The project submission period ran from 11 November 2020 to 10 March 2021. The call status states that the selection has been completed. Both dates are well in the past relative to today (2026-04-23).

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The call is framed as a grant competition for project ideas and outlines, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No mandatory co-financing or cost-sharing requirement is stated in the funding information or funding priorities documents.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No pre-existing accreditation with another body (e.g. GEF, Green Climate Fund) is required.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The document references consortium requirements but the truncated Section 6 does not explicitly state whether consortium leadership must be held by a non-UN entity.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The call is open to international implementing organisations and consortia; it is not restricted to entities based in Germany or the donor country.

results.proposal_summary: The IKI Thematic Call 2020, funded by the German Federal Ministry for the Environment, Nature Conservation and Nuclear Safety (BMU) through the International Climate Initiative (IKI), provided grants of EUR 5 to 30 million per project for initiatives aligned with 13 thematic priorities covering climate change mitigation, adaptation, biodiversity conservation, and sustainable economic recovery in the context of COVID-19. Projects were required to be implemented in ODA-eligible countries, with a preference for regional approaches covering up to five countries. Eligible implementing organisations were required to form consortia meeting specific criteria set out in Section 6 of the funding information. The call followed a two-stage process: project outlines were submitted between 11 November 2020 and 10 March 2021, and selected consortia were then invited to submit detailed proposals. The call is now closed, with 14 project outlines selected from 248 submissions. The ILO does not clearly fit within the defined eligible implementing organisation categories, and the submission deadline has long passed.

results.proposal_summary: The IKI Thematic Call 2017/2018 was a competitive call for project ideas issued by the German Federal Ministry for the Environment (BMUB) under the International Climate Initiative (IKI). It invited proposals for joint regional or national programmes addressing ten thematic priorities related to climate change mitigation, adaptation, and biodiversity conservation in IKI partner countries across Africa, Asia, Latin America, the Caribbean, and the Middle East. Programme funding volumes ranged from EUR 5 to 20 million each (generally EUR 15–20 million). Eligible applicants were consortia led by state partner institutions or international non-governmental organisations. The submission window ran from 20 July 2017 to 17 October 2017, and the selection process has since been completed with 13 outlines selected from 267 submissions. This call is closed and no longer accepting applications.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses on climate change mitigation, adaptation, and biodiversity conservation rather than on labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work, resulting in very low alignment with the ILO mandate.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The call is open only to invited non-governmental organisations as lead applicants. While consortia may include multilateral/intergovernmental organisations, the ILO cannot serve as lead applicant, and the call is explicitly restricted to invited NGOs as leads. Additionally, this is a Stage 2 by-invitation-only call, meaning the first required step (receiving an invitation in November 2025) has already passed.

Only invited NGOs may lead applications; ILO as an international organization is ineligible as lead applicant. The deadline is listed as 'Ongoing' but is a restricted/invited process where invitations were issued in November 2025. ILO could only participate as a consortium member, not as lead. The call is not aligned with ILO's core mandate of labour standards and decent work.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call explicitly states: 'This call is only open to invited non-governmental organisations as lead applicants.' ILO (an international/intergovernmental organization) is listed only as an eligible consortium participant, not as an eligible lead applicant. The eligible lead applicant category is strictly NGOs.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: Norad calls for proposals are non-reimbursable development grants. The page references the Grants Portal and grant management, indicating this is a grant instrument.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No specific grant size per project is mentioned in the page text. The overall programme has a 1 billion NOK ambition for 2025-2028, but individual grant amounts are not specified.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline is listed as 'Ongoing' with no specific date. However, this is a by-invitation-only Stage 2 process where invitations were issued in November 2025 (past). The actual submission deadline is not publicly stated.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a call for proposals for development grants, not a procurement tender or consultancy RFP.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The page does not explicitly mention co-financing requirements. The full call text is only available to invited applicants.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is mentioned as a requirement.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The call explicitly states it is 'only open to invited non-governmental organisations as lead applicants,' meaning a UN/intergovernmental body like ILO cannot lead a consortium.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO (as a multilateral/international organization) does not clearly fit the eligible applicant categories as described. While the grant scheme rules (Document 1) list 'Multilateral organisations' as eligible, the CfP focuses on information integrity and media resilience in Moldova, which is outside ILO's mandate on labour, employment, and social protection. More critically, this topic (media/information environment) has no alignment with ILO's mandate. However, assessing purely on eligibility: multilateral organisations ARE listed. But also, the theme (information integrity, media resilience) has essentially no ILO mandate alignment. Let me re-evaluate the ILO Eligibility criterion specifically: the regulatory framework (Document 1) explicitly lists 'Multilaterale organisasjoner' (Multilateral organisations) as eligible. The ILO is a multilateral organization, so it technically qualifies. However the content/theme of this CfP is entirely outside ILO's mandate. Setting eligible=false based on near-zero thematic relevance to ILO's work would not be correct per the task instructions - I should base eligibility on the hard criteria only. Re-checking: deadline is 2026-05-05 which is future. Funding is a grant. Grant size: not stated but the budget template covers years 2026-2029 (4 years), suggesting substantial funding well above $50k. ILO is a multilateral organization which is listed as eligible. So all hard criteria pass - eligible=true.

However, the thematic focus (media resilience, information environment, editorial independence) has essentially no alignment with ILO's mandate on labour, employment, and social protection.

The implementation period spans 2026-2029 (up to 4 years) based on the budget template, though no specific grant size is stated. Eligible applicants include multilateral organisations, Norwegian and foreign NGOs, commercial organisations, and state organisations. All applicants must be legal entities and demonstrate technical and administrative competence. Applications must be submitted via the Grants Portal by 5 May 2026. Co-financing is not mandatory. Indirect costs are capped at 7% of direct operational costs.

results.exclusion_reason: Deadline has passed: the submission deadline of 6/3/2026 (interpreted as March 6, 2026 in DD/MM/YYYY Norwegian date format, or confirmed as past given the publication date context) — however re-evaluating: the publication date '4/8/2026' in Norwegian format would be April 8, 2026, which aligns with today. If the deadline '6/3/2026' is in Norwegian DD/MM/YYYY format, it would be June 3, 2026 (future). But 6 March 2026 would be before publication date of 8 April 2026, making no sense. Thus the deadline '6/3/2026' = June 3, 2026, which is in the future relative to today (April 22, 2026).

results.proposal_summary: Norway (via Norad and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs) is allocating 100 million NOK for private sector development in Ukraine in 2026 and 2027, under the Nansen Support Programme for Ukraine (budget item 159.73). The call aims to promote investment-ready projects, facilitate increased private investment in Ukraine, mobilise private capital, create new jobs, and improve framework conditions for the private sector. Priority sectors include sustainable energy production, food security and agriculture, and reconstruction of critical infrastructure. The call is open to all applicants from any country (Ukrainian, international, and Norwegian), including multilateral organisations, non-profit organisations, commercial organisations, and state organisations that are legal entities with demonstrated technical and administrative competence. Applications must be submitted via the Grants Portal by 17 June 2026. Co-financing is not mandatory but can be included. Indirect costs are capped at 7% of direct operational costs. There is no explicitly stated minimum grant size; the total pool is 100 million NOK across 2026-2027.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call explicitly targets private sector development and job creation in Ukraine—which touches on ILO mandate areas of employment and enterprise development—it is primarily focused on investment mobilisation and commercial enterprise rather than labour standards, social protection, social dialogue, or core decent work elements that are central to the ILO's mandate.

results.proposal_summary: Global Affairs Canada (GAC) operates a rolling unsolicited concept note mechanism for international assistance grants aligned with Canada's Feminist International Assistance Policy. There is no fixed submission deadline; organizations submit concept notes at any time through the Partners@International portal. If a concept note is of interest, GAC invites the applicant to submit a full proposal. Eligible applicants are legal entities (Canadian organizations must have a CRA number); UN agencies and international organizations are not explicitly included. No grant size minimum or maximum is specified, and amounts must be quoted in Canadian dollars. The geographic scope of funded projects is global (developing countries). The two-stage process involves a concept note followed by a full proposal invitation; funding is not guaranteed until a contribution agreement is signed. The ILO, as a UN intergovernmental organization, does not clearly fit the implied eligible applicant categories.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While GAC's Feminist International Assistance Policy can encompass labour rights, gender equality at work, and social protection themes relevant to ILO's mandate, the funding mechanism is primarily designed for NGOs and legal entities rather than UN agencies, and the CfP does not specifically target ILO core themes such as labour standards, employment policy, or social dialogue.

The page does not constitute an active Call for Proposals.

results.classification_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) is a Canadian government grant program funding small, high-impact projects in over 120 ODA-eligible countries. Several country-specific calls remain open with future deadlines (earliest open deadline: 2026-04-21 for Cambodia, which has already passed, but multiple others remain open through April–May 2026). The average grant is ~$31,000 CAD, which is below the USD 50,000 threshold. However, the program description does not state a fixed maximum and individual grants could exceed USD 50,000 depending on the country call. Given ambiguity, the threshold criterion is marked unknown. The funding is non-reimbursable (grant). Overall the ILO can apply as an intergovernmental organization.

results.proposal_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI), funded by the Government of Canada with an annual budget of CAD 26.8 million, supports approximately 650 small, high-impact projects annually in over 120 ODA-eligible countries. Projects average CAD 31,000 each and focus on inclusive governance, gender equality, peace and security, economic growth, environmental action, and human dignity. Eligible applicants include local NGOs, community groups, non-profits, academic institutions, international NGOs, intergovernmental and multilateral organizations (including the ILO), regional organizations, recipient-country government agencies, and Canadian NGOs. Applications are managed by Canadian Embassies and High Commissions in each country, with country-specific calls and deadlines; multiple calls are currently open through April–May 2026. No co-financing is required. The ILO, as an intergovernmental organization, may apply in partnership with local organizations.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The CFLI covers themes relevant to ILO's mandate including fair jobs, women's economic rights, entrepreneurship, and decent work, but the program primarily targets small local civil society organizations with modest grants averaging ~CAD 31,000, which is well below ILO's typical project scale, limiting practical alignment.

results.proposal_summary: The Swedish International Development Cooperation Agency (Sida), on behalf of the Swedish government, launched a Call for Expression of Interest in November 2024 to identify Central Operational Partners for multi-year humanitarian partnerships under Sweden's Humanitarian Assistance Strategy (2025-2029). The process was open to non-profit NGOs, UN agencies, Red Cross/Red Crescent entities, and global humanitarian pooled funds with operations in at least 15 crisis countries. The objective is to save lives, alleviate suffering, and uphold human dignity in humanitarian crises worldwide, with a focus on effectiveness, transparency, and reaching the most vulnerable. No specific grant amount is stated. The first-stage deadline expired on 10 November 2024; 25 organisations were shortlisted and are now undergoing external assessment and appraisal. New applications are no longer accepted.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call concerns humanitarian assistance in crisis contexts—a topic tangentially related to ILO's work on decent work in crisis situations—the primary focus is on life-saving humanitarian operations, protection, and emergency response rather than ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, or social protection.

results.proposal_summary: This page from Sida (Sweden's development cooperation agency) provides an informational update — not an active call for proposals — regarding the CSO Strategy 2025–2029. The update clarifies three key decisions: party-affiliated organisations (PAOs) are excluded from eligibility under this strategy; own funds (15% co-financing requirement) must consist solely of privately raised resources; and three-year agreements with extension options are permitted despite the strategy's flexibility requirements. The first call for proposals was conducted in 2024, and applicants were informed of indicative amounts and contract lengths in June 2025. A new call for major support is planned for 2027 with agreements expected from 2028. Eligible applicants are civil society organisations only; international organisations such as the ILO are not eligible. No active submission deadline exists.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While Sida's CSO strategy broadly supports civil society and development cooperation that may touch on ILO-related themes such as rights and equality, this call is exclusively for civil society organisations and does not align with ILO's mandate or eligibility as an applicant.

Additionally, the first mandatory submission deadline (Expression of Interest) closed on 27 May 2024, which is in the past.
results.classification_summary: This Sida call for expressions of interest is restricted exclusively to civil society organisations as defined by the OECD/DAC, including community-based organisations, NGOs, faith-based organisations, labour unions, and similar non-state, non-market entities. The ILO, as a UN intergovernmental organisation, does not fall within any of the listed eligible categories. The call is therefore ineligible for the ILO on two independent grounds.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call explicitly defines eligible applicants as civil society organisations (CSOs) using the OECD/DAC definition: non-market, non-state organisations including community-based organisations, NGOs, faith-based organisations, labour unions, cooperatives, professional associations, etc. The ILO, as a UN intergovernmental organisation, is not a CSO and does not fit any of the three listed eligible categories.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The call provides non-reimbursable financial support (grants/contributions) to civil society organisations under the Swedish CSO Strategy 2025–2029. No repayment obligation is mentioned.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Applications under parts 1 and 2 together amount to just over SEK 9 billion across all applicants, indicating individual grants well above USD 50,000. Small-scale grants are also mentioned for Swedish organisations but no indication they fall below USD 50,000.

Deadline is future.evidence: The first mandatory submission stage (Expression of Interest / Step 1) closed on 27 May 2024, which is before today's date of 2026-04-23. The process is described as closed for new applicants.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a call for expressions of interest and proposals for development cooperation grants, not a procurement or tender for goods/services.

results.proposal_summary: Sweden's development cooperation agency Sida launched a call for expressions of interest under the Strategy for Sweden's Development Cooperation with Civil Society 2025–2029. The call, which closed on 27 May 2024, targeted civil society organisations globally — including those based in ODA countries, those forwarding funds to CSOs in ODA countries, and non-ODA-based organisations working on enabling environments for civil society. The strategy aims to strengthen the capacity of civil society in developing countries and improve their operating environment. Support agreements are three years in duration (activity periods until December 2028), with a possibility of extension. Applications under Parts 1 and 2 together total over SEK 9 billion. Organisations outside ODA countries must contribute own funds of at least 15% of Sida's contribution. The ILO is ineligible as it is not a civil society organisation and the initial deadline has passed.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While some CSO activities supported (labour unions, democracy, human rights) overlap tangentially with ILO mandate areas, this call is specifically designed for civil society organisations and focuses on civil society strengthening rather than core ILO themes of labour standards, employment, and social protection.

results.proposal_summary: The Danida Fellowship Centre, on behalf of the Danish Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Danida), issued a two-phase call for development research grant applications for 2026. Eligible applicants are exclusively universities and research institutions in Denmark. Grants of up to 10 million DKK support collaborative research projects of up to five years' duration, addressing thematic priorities under Denmark's development cooperation strategies ('A Changing World – Partnerships in Development' and 'Africa's Century'), with a focus on Danida priority partner countries in the developing world. Phase 1 (prequalification) is now closed; those prequalified are invited to submit full proposals in Phase 2, with funded projects expected to start in April 2027. The ILO is ineligible as a direct applicant due to the strict restriction to Danish universities and research institutions.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call funds development research broadly aligned with sustainable development, which may tangentially touch ILO-relevant themes (employment, social protection, just transition), but the primary focus is academic research in Danish institutions with no specific mandate linked to ILO's core areas of labour standards, social dialogue, or decent work.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The call targets research institutions/universities in Denmark and partner countries; ILO as an international organization does not fit the eligible applicant categories. Additionally, the eligible applicant types exclude ILO.

The eligible applicants are research institutions and universities, not international organizations such as the ILO. Because the ILO does not fit within the exhaustive list of eligible entity types defined by this call, it is ineligible. No explicit submission deadline date is found in the provided content, making the deadline status unknown, but the ILO eligibility exclusion alone is sufficient to render this call ineligible.

results.proposal_summary: The Danida Fellowship Centre, on behalf of the Danish Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Danida), invites Phase 2 full proposals for development research grants under the 2025 Research Grants Call. The call supports genuine collaborative research projects between Danish universities/research institutions (North) and partner-country research institutions (South) in Danida's priority developing countries. Projects must address the call's thematic focus, generate new knowledge, contribute to the SDGs, and include substantial research capacity strengthening in the South. Eligible applicants are Danish-led research consortia with Southern partners; international organizations such as the ILO are not eligible. The grant amount is not specified in available content. No explicit submission deadline is visible in the provided text. The call requires equitable North-South partnerships, gender sensitivity, and relevance to Danish development cooperation priorities.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call supports development research that may touch on labour, social protection, or decent work themes, it is a research-focused academic grant restricted to university/research institution applicants and does not align with ILO's operational mandate or eligible applicant profile.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The CfP explicitly lists eligible applicant types as non-profit organizations, non-governmental organizations, and commercial entities. UN agencies/international organizations are not listed as eligible applicants in the main categories, though a separate UN entity self-certification form exists as an annex. However, the core selection criteria and eligibility focus on organizations with evaluation/research experience in AgTech/FinTech for rural smallholders, and the text describes 'Non-profit organizations, non-governmental organizations, and commercial entities are welcome to apply' as the eligible categories. The ILO does not clearly fit into these listed categories as an implementing partner applicant. Additionally, the co-financing requirement (minimum 20% for non-private sector, 25% for private sector) is a mandatory condition.
results.classification_summary: This IFAD Call for Proposals seeks an implementing partner for a grant to generate evidence on AgTech and FinTech solutions for rural smallholders through the Innovatech programme. Eligible applicants are non-profit organizations, NGOs, and commercial entities. The ILO, as a UN/international organization, does not clearly fit the listed eligible applicant categories. The deadline is 13 May 2026, which is in the future. Co-financing of at least 20% is mandatory. The grant is non-reimbursable. Grant size is not explicitly stated but appears above USD 50,000 based on project scope. The CfP is flagged for mandatory co-financing and has consortium leadership rules (lead must be the proponent institution, with partners sub-contracted).

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The CfP states 'Non-profit organizations, non-governmental organizations, and commercial entities are welcome to apply.' ILO as a UN specialized agency/international organization does not clearly fit into these listed categories. While a UN entity self-certification form exists (Annex 2), the eligible applicant list does not include UN agencies or international organizations as a distinct eligible category for this implementing partner role.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The CfP explicitly refers to a 'grant' throughout, including 'IFAD grant resources,' 'grant financing,' and 'Grant Concept Note.' This is a non-reimbursable grant instrument.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The specific grant amount is not stated in the available documents. However, given the scope of the project (multi-country evaluation across LAC and NEN regions, multiple years, with 20% co-financing requirement), the grant is likely well above USD 50,000. Cannot confirm with certainty from available text.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline for submitting applications is 13 May 2026 Rome time (UTC +2), which is after today's date of 2026-04-22.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a Call for Proposals for a grant implementing partner, not a procurement tender for goods/services. It uses grant terminology and grant agreement frameworks throughout.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The ToR explicitly states: 'all proposals should include at least 20% of co-financing (in-cash and/or in-kind). Proposals from for-profit Private Sector entities should include at least 25% of co-financing (in-cash and/or in-kind).' Co-financing is a mandatory compliance requirement with minimum recipient contribution requirements.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required. Eligibility is based on self-certification and IFAD's own due diligence processes.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The LoI states: 'only the proponent institution shall be responsible for achieving the project objectives and outcomes...while the partner institution(s) will be sub-contracted by the proponent.' The lead/proponent institution does not need to be a non-UN entity per se, but leadership rules are present requiring a single lead proponent.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: No restriction to applicants from any specific country. The call is open internationally to non-profit organizations, NGOs, and commercial entities without geographic restriction on applicant registration.

results.proposal_summary: IFAD is seeking an implementing partner for a grant titled 'Evidence for Scale-Up of AgTech and FinTech Solutions through IFAD's PoLG' (Reference No. 2000005624), published on 21 April 2026. The grant aims to generate robust evidence and lessons learned to support the expansion of financial and non-financial services for rural smallholders delivered by AgTech and FinTech companies under IFAD's Innovatech programme, including ex-post evaluation in Latin America and the Caribbean (Innovatech 1.0 and 2.0) and ex-ante assessment in Near East, North Africa, Europe, and Central Asia (Jordan, Uzbekistan, Turkey). The implementing partner will also develop an investor-oriented impact reporting methodology. Eligible applicants are non-profit organizations, NGOs, and commercial entities; UN agencies/international organizations are not listed as eligible. All proposals must include at least 20% co-financing (25% for private sector), and grant budget amounts are not specified. The deadline for submission is 13 May 2026 (Rome time, UTC+2). A Q&A session is scheduled for 4 May 2026 (per LoI) or 6 May 2026 (per ToR). Proposals must be submitted in English to lacgrants@ifad.org.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This CfP focuses on AgTech/FinTech solutions, rural smallholder agriculture, and impact evaluation in the context of rural development, which has limited alignment with the ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment policy, social protection, and decent work.

The grant aims to promote inclusive, sustainable, and climate-resilient agricultural value chains, strengthen farmers' organizations (FOs) capacity for service delivery, policy dialogue, and governance. Eligible applicants are limited to FOs representing more than one country with a mandate in North Africa, the Balkans, or Turkey, or non-profit entities (individually or in consortium with an FO) with at least five years of experience working with smallholder farmers in the region. The grant amount is not specified in available documentation. The re-advertised submission deadline was 29 December 2025, which has passed. No mandatory co-financing requirement was identified.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The CfP focuses on agricultural value chains, farmers' organizations, and smallholder farmers' market access and policy engagement, which is tangentially related to ILO themes of employment and rural livelihoods but does not primarily address labour standards, social protection, social dialogue, or decent work as defined by the ILO mandate.

results.proposal_summary: The Australian Government Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade (DFAT) has issued an Expression of Interest (DFAT 1091) on AusTender for procurement of management advisory services to implement the Primary Health Care for Papua New Guinea (PHC4PNG) program. The program aims to support PNG in delivering its National Health Plan 2021-2030 by strengthening primary health care systems, governance, and services, particularly in rural and remote areas. It covers communicable diseases, maternal and child health, and non-communicable diseases, with a health innovation fund component. The implementation period is December 2026 to December 2031 with a 5-year extension option. This is a procurement tender (service contract), not a grant, with the EOI closing on 5 May 2026. No estimated contract value is stated. The ILO is not eligible as this is a procurement exercise for management advisory services.

results.proposal_summary: The United Nations Road Safety Fund (UNRSF) launched its 2025 Call for Proposals on 4 July 2025 with USD 2 million allocated to fund projects addressing two priority areas: motorcycle safety and road crash data collection and analysis. Only UNRSF participating organisations (PUNOs and NUNOs), including ILO, WHO, UNDP, UNICEF, and approximately 20 other UN agencies and international organisations, are eligible to submit concept notes directly; other entities (NGOs, academic institutions, private sector) must partner with a participating organisation. Projects must align with the Safe Systems Approach and the Global Framework Plan of Action for Road Safety, addressing gaps identified in the 2023 Global Status Report. The call was open from 4 July to 5 October 2025, with no co-financing requirement stated. The implementation period is not specified. The call is global in scope, with a preference for high-burden countries.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While road safety touches on occupational safety in transport sectors and ILO is listed as a participating organisation, the call's core focus on road traffic safety (motorcycle safety and crash data) falls largely outside the ILO's primary mandate of labour standards, employment, and social protection.

results.proposal_summary: The Italian Agency for International Cooperation and Development (AICS), through its Tirana office, is launching a grant call for the initiative 'SHAR DINARIC - Sustainable Development and Protection of Natural and Cultural Heritage in the Transborder Dinaric Alps' (AID 013071/01/0). The initiative is divided into two lots: Lot 1 (€1,500,000) for sustainable tourism development in the Dinaric Alps transborder area in North Macedonia, and Lot 2 (€1,500,000) for the same in Kosovo, for a total budget of €3,000,000. Eligible applicants are exclusively Civil Society Organizations (OSC) and non-profit entities registered on the Italian list under Article 26(3) of Law 125/2014, either as single applicants or in temporary consortia (ATS). The initiative aims to develop sustainable tourism, protect natural and cultural heritage, and improve socioeconomic conditions of local communities in the Shar Mountain National Parks area. The deadline for submission of executive proposals is 1 July 2026 at 13:00. Co-financing is not mandatory. The ILO is not eligible as it does not meet the strict applicant eligibility requirements.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The initiative focuses primarily on sustainable tourism and environmental heritage protection in the Dinaric Alps, with limited connection to ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment, social protection, and social dialogue, though there are marginal links to employment creation and just transitions.

results.proposal_summary: AICS Jerusalem, under the EPEC programme (AID 11399) for improvement of the Palestinian business environment, issued a Call for Expression of Interest to select up to 5 Palestinian tech companies/entities to participate in the WMF – We Make Future 2026 international trade fair in Bologna, Italy (24–26 June 2026). Selected participants receive travel, accommodation, meals, and insurance from AICS, as well as a collective exhibition space from WMF organizers. Eligible applicants must be legally registered in Palestine, operating in technology, digital, AI, software, or related sectors, with at least 2 years of operational history and a market-ready or near-ready product/service. The submission deadline was 19 April 2026 at 23:59 Jerusalem time, which has already passed. No monetary grant is awarded; support is in-kind for event participation only.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses on Palestinian private tech sector enterprise development and internationalisation, with tangential relevance to ILO themes of sustainable enterprises and employment creation, but has no direct connection to labour standards, social protection, social dialogue, or other core ILO mandate areas.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The guidelines define a strict and exhaustive list of eligible lead applicants (NGOs, private companies, public sector operators, local authorities, or inter-governmental organizations as defined by Article 159(1) of the EU Financial Regulation). While ILO as an inter-governmental organization could technically fall under Article 159(1), the guidelines also require the lead applicant to be 'registered towards the relevant authority in Uganda i.e. legitimated to fully operate in Uganda' and 'effectively established in a Member State of the European Union or in an eligible country or territory as defined under Article 28(1) of NDICI-GE' — though the latter obligation does not apply to international organisations. However, more critically, the guidelines require the lead applicant to demonstrate at least 7 years of experience in incubation and acceleration of MSMEs preferably in NTFPs Value Chains and/or Rural Development, and the capacity to manage a yearly project portfolio of at least EUR 1,000,000. These are specialized sectoral requirements (non-timber forest products value chains, MSME incubation) outside the ILO's core mandate. Additionally, the CfP is focused on forest conservation and non-timber value chain enterprise development — a domain not squarely within ILO's mandate. The primary exclusion reason is that while ILO could be considered an inter-governmental organization eligible in principle, the operational eligibility requirements (NTFP/MSME incubation expertise, Uganda registration) make ILO an implausible fit, and the ILO match score is very low.
results.classification_summary: This is a restricted Call for Proposals by AICS (Italian Agency for Development Cooperation) Kampala office, funded by the EU under the ReForEst Uganda project (NDICI AFRICA/2023/450-049). It seeks one grantee to implement Output 3 of the ReForEst Uganda project: developing sustainable non-timber forest product (NTFP) value chain enterprises in Eastern Uganda and Napak District, Karamoja. The grant range is EUR 3,000,000 to EUR 4,000,000. Eligible lead applicants must be NGOs, private companies, public sector operators, local authorities, or inter-governmental organizations registered in Uganda with at least 7 years of MSME incubation/acceleration experience. The deadline for concept note submission is 7 May 2026. While ILO could qualify as an inter-governmental organization under Article 159(1) of the EU Financial Regulation, the highly specialized eligibility requirements (NTFP/MSME incubation experience, Uganda registration) and the thematic focus (forest conservation, non-timber value chains) make this opportunity a poor fit for ILO. The ILO match score is very low.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The guidelines (art. 2.1.1) define eligible lead applicants as NGOs, private companies, public sector operators, local authorities, or international (inter-governmental) organizations as defined by Article 159(1) of the EU Financial Regulation — registered in Uganda and legitimated to fully operate there. While ILO is an inter-governmental organization, the guidelines also require a minimum of 7 years' experience in incubation and acceleration of MSMEs preferably in NTFPs Value Chains and/or Rural Development, and demonstration of capacity to manage a yearly project portfolio of at least EUR 1,000,000. These specialized sectoral requirements are outside ILO's mandate and expertise, effectively excluding it from eligibility as a lead applicant. ILO is not explicitly listed as an eligible entity type in the context of this specialized call.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The document explicitly states this is a Call for Proposals for a non-reimbursable grant, with amounts between EUR 3,000,000 and EUR 4,000,000.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The minimum grant amount is EUR 3,000,000 and maximum is EUR 4,000,000, both well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline for submission of concept notes is May 7th, 2026 at 16:00 EAT, which is in the future relative to today's date of April 22, 2026.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a Call for Proposals for a grant, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The guidelines state the grant may cover up to 100% of eligible costs. Co-financing is not mandatory; the guidelines note that if full financing is requested, the applicant must justify it, but it is not excluded.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body (e.g., GEF, Green Climate Fund) is required. Note that lead applicants whose pillars have been positively assessed by the European Commission may sign a contribution agreement instead of a standard grant contract, but this is not a prerequisite for applying.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The guidelines do not specify that consortium leadership must be held by a non-UN entity. The lead applicant categories include inter-governmental organizations.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Lead applicants must be effectively established in a Member State of the European Union or in an eligible country or territory as defined under Article 28(1) of NDICI-GE (though this does not apply to international organizations), and must be registered/legitimated to fully operate in Uganda.

results.proposal_summary: The Italian Agency for Development Cooperation (AICS), acting as contracting authority under the EU-funded ReForEst Uganda project (NDICI AFRICA/2023/450-049, total budget EUR 14 million), is issuing a restricted Call for Proposals (ReForEst/CFP/2026/03) to identify one grantee to implement Output 3 of the project: developing sustainable non-timber forest product (NTFP) value chain enterprises and strategies for enhanced livelihoods in Uganda's Eastern Region and Napak District (Karamoja). The grant amount ranges from EUR 3,000,000 to EUR 4,000,000, covering up to 100% of eligible costs. Eligible lead applicants must be NGOs, private companies, public sector operators, local authorities, or inter-governmental organizations, registered and legitimated to operate in Uganda, and effectively established in an EU Member State or NDICI-GE eligible country (exception applies to international organizations). Lead applicants must have at least 7 years of MSME incubation/acceleration experience in NTFP value chains and/or rural development. The call follows a two-stage process: concept notes are due by 7 May 2026 at 16:00 EAT, with up to 5 pre-selected applicants later invited to submit full proposals. The action targets 16 districts across Eastern Uganda and must promote 5 NTFP value chains (cashew, wild mango, honey, sheanut, bamboo), incubate 140 enterprises, support 50 production groups, and sustain 5,000 jobs.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This CfP is primarily focused on forest conservation, non-timber forest product value chain development, and MSME incubation in Uganda — areas peripheral to the ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment policy, social protection, and social dialogue, with only minor overlap in job creation and rural livelihoods.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The CfP explicitly restricts eligible applicants to non-profit entities (soggetti non profit / OSC - Organizzazioni della Società Civile) registered in Italy or in the partner country (Sudan). The FAQ further clarifies that lead applicants must be OSCs registered in the AICS list pursuant to Article 26 of Law 125/2014, which covers civil society organizations, not UN agencies.

The FAQ confirms lead applicants must be OSCs registered in this list. The ILO as an international/intergovernmental organization is not included in these eligible categories.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The CfP provides non-reimbursable contributions (grants) to selected non-profit organizations for emergency project implementation in Sudan. The document refers to 'finanziamento dei progetti di emergenza' (financing of emergency projects) as grants.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: Applicants must be registered in the AICS list of OSCs pursuant to Article 26, paragraph 3, of Law 125/2014 (Italian law on development cooperation). This constitutes a specific pre-existing accreditation/registration requirement.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The FAQ specifies that if a local organization (developing country-based NGO) capable of demonstrating operational capacity is part of an ATS (consortium), it must assume the role of lead entity (Lead Partner), under penalty of exclusion. This restricts consortium leadership to non-UN entities.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: Eligible lead applicants must be OSCs registered in Italy (in the AICS list pursuant to Art. 26 of Law 125/2014) or, in specific consortium cases, local organizations registered in Sudan. The ILO as a UN/international intergovernmental organisation does not fit this category.

results.proposal_summary: The Italian Agency for Development Cooperation (AICS), through its Addis Abeba office, has issued a Call for Proposals (AID 013303/01/0) for a EUR 10.4 million humanitarian response initiative in Ethiopia titled 'Rafforzamento della risposta umanitaria in Etiopia.' The initiative is structured across three components: Component 1 (EUR 5.2M) addresses conflict-related needs in northern Ethiopia (Tigray, Afar, Amhara); Component 2 (EUR 1.5M) responds to the complex South Sudanese and Sudanese crisis and displacement in Gambella and Benishangul Gumuz; and Component 3 (EUR 3.7M) addresses extreme climate phenomena in Oromia, Somali, South West, South Ethiopia, Central, and Sidama regions. Eligible applicants are Italian non-profit civil society organisations (OSC) inscribed in the AICS official list per art. 26, with individual grants capped at EUR 700,000 per single organisation and EUR 1,500,000 for joint ATS consortia. No co-financing requirement is specified. The submission deadline has been extended to 27 April 2026 at 17:00 (Addis Abeba time). The ILO is not eligible as it is an international organisation, not an Italian-registered non-profit entity.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While this CfP addresses humanitarian response in Ethiopia, which has tangential links to ILO's work on decent work in crisis situations, it is primarily focused on food security, health, nutrition, WASH and protection activities rather than core ILO mandate areas such as labour standards, employment, social protection or social dialogue.

results.proposal_summary: The ASSIST Facility 2.0, managed by the Joint SDG Fund, is a blended finance investment vehicle targeting $150M to catalyse up to $10 billion in sustainable SDG-aligned investments in Indonesia by 2030. Rather than a grant, it is structured as a financial instrument offering 6-8% yield returns with 10:1–15:1 leverage through thematic bond issuance (including sovereign blue bonds and municipal sukuk) and venture capital deployment over a 5-year period. Eligible participants are financial investors including sovereign wealth funds, development finance institutions, Islamic finance institutions, and corporate impact investors. results.classification_summary: This is the TriNova 2026 Call for Triangular Cooperation Projects, a joint initiative by APC Colombia and Germany (GIZ/BMZ). It funds triangular cooperation projects up to EUR 300,000 for up to 2 years, involving at least one Latin American/Caribbean partner. Eligible applicants are explicitly listed as public institutions, private institutions, civil society, academia, and NGOs — ILO as an international organization is not included. The deadline is 08 May 2026. ILO is ineligible due to the restrictive applicant eligibility criteria.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The guide explicitly states eligible participants are: 'instituciones públicas a nivel nacional o local, instituciones privadas, sociedad civil, academia y organismos no gubernamentales.' International organizations such as the ILO (a UN agency) are not included in this exhaustive list.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: TriNova contributes up to EUR 300,000 (cash) per project as a non-reimbursable contribution. The document states 'no hay transferencia de fondos' between partners but the TriNova contribution is a grant/non-repayable financial support.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: TriNova contributes up to 300,000 euros per project, which exceeds the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The call closes on 08 May 2026, which is after today's date of 22 April 2026.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a project grant call for triangular cooperation proposals, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The guide states each partner (requesting partner and main partner) must provide their own contribution (in kind or financial): 'Por parte del socio solicitante, se espera un aporte adecuado y pertinente para el cumplimiento de los objetivos del proyecto.' Co-financing from all partners is a mandatory condition.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required. However, at least one team member must have completed the GIZ Triangular Cooperation Project Formulation course.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No explicit rule requiring consortium leadership by a non-UN entity is stated; the facilitating role is taken by TriNova (APC Colombia + GIZ), and other partners take on requesting or main partner roles.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The second partner must belong to Latin America, the Caribbean, or another region of the Global South, and Colombia's APC plays a central role as co-facilitator. Projects must be aligned to Colombia's National Development Plan. While not strictly limited to one country, the geographic focus is significantly restricted to the LAC/Global South region.

results.proposal_summary: The U.S. Department of State's Bureau of Democracy, Human Rights, and Labor (DRL) issued an Annual Program Statement in October 2024 inviting Statements of Interest (SOIs) from organizations implementing Internet Freedom programs. The program supports four themes: (1) directly circumventing or defeating internet censorship through technology; (2) preventing and remedying online attacks through digital security; (3) advocating against repressive internet laws; and (4) researching and documenting threats to internet freedom. Priority regions include Africa, East Asia and the Pacific, North Africa and the Middle East, South and Central Asia, and the Western Hemisphere. No specific grant size is stated. Programs are expected to begin no earlier than August or September 2025. Eligible applicants are civil society organizations, NGOs, and implementing partners; the ILO as an international organization does not fit the eligible applicant category. The SOI submission deadline is not explicitly stated in the available text.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This CfP focuses exclusively on internet freedom, anti-censorship technology, and digital security, which fall outside the ILO's core mandate on labour standards, employment, social protection, and decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The U.S. Department of State's Bureau of Democracy, Human Rights and Labor (DRL) has issued standing Proposal Submission Instructions (updated January 2020) governing applications to its grant programs. Eligible applicants include U.S.- and foreign-based NGOs, public international organizations (including the ILO), academic institutions, and for-profit entities, with a preference for non-profits. The instructions apply globally across individual Notices of Funding Opportunities (NOFOs) issued by DRL, each of which will specify countries, thematic focus, funding amounts, and deadlines. Funding is provided as non-reimbursable assistance awards (grants), and co-financing is explicitly not required. Applications are submitted through the SAMS Domestic portal and must include detailed narratives, budgets, logic models, monitoring and evaluation plans, risk analyses, and gender analyses. No overarching grant size or implementation period is specified in these instructions.

results.proposal_summary: The U.S. Department of State's Office to Monitor and Combat Trafficking in Persons (TIP Office) runs an annual competitive grant program funding programs to combat human trafficking globally (outside the United States). Eligible applicants include U.S.-based and foreign NGOs, non-profits, for-profit organizations (no profit generation permitted), higher education institutions, public international organizations (including UN agencies such as the ILO), and U.S. government agencies; foreign governments are excluded. Awards have typically reached up to USD 1,000,000 for two to three years, with larger and longer-term proposals welcomed. The two-stage process starts with a Statement of Interest in response to a NOFO posted on SAMS Domestic and Grants.gov; no specific deadline is stated on the general process page. Projects must address prosecution, protection, or prevention of sex and/or labor trafficking, incorporate survivor input, and align with U.S. government priorities. Contact: JTIPGrants@state.gov.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The grant directly targets labor trafficking and forced labour—core ILO mandate areas under fundamental principles and rights at work—and the ILO as a PIO is explicitly eligible to apply.

results.proposal_summary: The Agence Française de Développement (AFD) has launched a call for initiatives for the implementation of an 'urban nursery' (Pépinière urbaine) in Bouaké, Côte d'Ivoire, as part of the broader Cultures en Côte d'Ivoire project. results.proposal_summary: The Agence Française de Développement (AFD), on behalf of the French Government, operates several grant financing mechanisms for civil society organizations (CSOs) and NGOs working in international solidarity and development. The main instruments include the CSO Initiatives Facility (for projects ≥ €500,000 in all OECD DAC ODA-eligible countries, co-financing up to 80–90% of project costs), the now-suspended FISONG innovation facility (open to international NGOs of any nationality, funding €700k–€1M per project), the Crisis and Post-Crisis Calls for Projects (APCC, funded via Minka Fund and EU trust funds), and the Support Fund for Feminist Organizations (FSOF). Eligible applicants are strictly CSOs/NGOs — French associations, syndicates, foundations, and local civil society organizations — explicitly excluding international organizations such as the ILO. All specific calls documented on this page have closed deadlines (2018–2022), and the FISONG has been suspended since 2023. The CSO Initiatives Facility reopens annually mid-March to mid-May but no confirmed 2026 active call is documented. Mandatory co-financing of at least 10% is required for most instruments.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While some past FISONG themes (social protection, financial inclusion of migrants, informal economy) align with ILO's mandate, the ILO is explicitly ineligible as an international institution under all AFD CSO financing mechanisms, making this opportunity irrelevant for ILO regardless of thematic alignment.
